figures.caption: Table 1: Error measures and physical diagnostics used in the numerical experiments.

Fig. 1: Profile comparison for Example 4.1. Subfigure (a) shows the phase-field profile φθ(x, t) against the reference solution. Subfigure (b) shows the concentration profile cθ(x, t) against the reference solution.

Fig. 2: Physical diagnostics for Example 4.1. Subfigure (a) compares the predicted corrosion depth dθ(t) with the reference depth dref(t). Subfigure (b) shows the tempered concentration-mass drift eλtMc,θ(t)−Mc,θ(0).

Table 4: Regime-dependent implementation details for Example 4.2. The remaining model functions, initial profiles, boundary conditions, optimizer, and neural representation are the same as in Example 4.1.

Fig. 3: Profile comparison for Example 4.2. Subfigures (a) and (b) show the phase-field profile φθ(x, t) and concentration profile cθ(x, t) in the activation-controlled regime. Subfigures (c), (d) show the corresponding profiles in the diffusion-controlled regime.

Fig. 4: Physical diagnostics for Example 4.2. Subfigures (a)–(c) show the corrosion depth d(t), interface concentration cΓ(t), and concentration peak cmax(t) in the activation-controlled regime. Subfigures (d)–(f) show the corresponding diagnostics in the diffusion-controlled regime.
Fig. 7: Geometry-based pitting diagnostics for Example 4.3. Subfigure (a) compares the pit area Apit(t). Subfigure (b) compares the equivalent pit radius Req(t).
Fig. 8: Parameter-identification history for Example 4.4. The curves show the learned mobilities Lmob,θ and Mmob,θ, while the horizontal reference lines indicate L†mob and M †mob.

Fig. 9: Post-identification field validation for Example 4.4. The reconstructed phasefield profile φθ(x, t) and concentration profile cθ(x, t) are compared with the reference solution at representative time levels.
